title: The Mechanism Behind Dominant vs. Recessive Traits

lesson.learn:
Dominant and recessive traits are patterns of gene expression, not labels for strength or importance. A dominant allele shows its effect when one copy is present, while a recessive allele usually shows only when two copies are inherited. This is why some traits appear in one generation and seem to vanish in the next.

The mechanism is simple in concept but powerful in practice. Genes interact with each other, and the phenotype we see depends on which version of a gene is present and how the cell reads it.
lesson.simpler: Dominant traits appear with one copy of an allele, while recessive traits usually need two copies to show.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Dominance is about expression, not value.
lesson.deeperDive:
Mendel's pea plants made these patterns easier to see because the traits were distinct and the genes acted in relatively simple ways. But real biology can be more complicated. Some genes show incomplete dominance, codominance, or many-gene interactions. Even so, the basic dominant-recessive idea is still useful because it explains a huge number of human and plant inheritance patterns.

Punnett squares help learners predict probabilities, but they do not guarantee outcomes. They are maps of chance, not fate.
lesson.funFact: A dominant allele does not mean a gene is better, only that it is more visible.

people: Gregor Mendel - the pea plant pioneer
Farmers and breeders - people who use inheritance patterns
places: Pea plant gardens - Mendel's classic experiments
Genetics classrooms - where inheritance is taught
Family lines - where traits are observed across generations
years: Present day - inheritance is taught using these basics
concepts: Allele - one version of a gene
Recessive - usually expressed with two copies
Punnett square - a probability chart for inheritance
